stories.title: 20 Trinamool MPs break from Mamata Banerjee to merge with a tiny party — a legal workaround to defect without losing their seats
stories.what_happened: A rebel group of 20 TMC Lok Sabha MPs — led by figures including Kakoli Ghosh Dastidar and Sudip Bandyopadhyay — informed Lok Sabha Speaker Om Birla they are merging with the Tripura-based Nationalist Citizens Party of India (NCPI) and asked to be recognised as a separate bloc. Because 20 of TMC's 28 MPs (more than two-thirds) are crossing over, the move is designed to satisfy the anti-defection rule. The government arranged for the group to attend the pre-session all-party meeting, prompting the wider opposition to stage a symbolic walkout.
key_points: 20 of TMC's 28 Lok Sabha MPs are quitting Mamata's leadership
They plan to merge into the little-known Nationalist Citizens Party (NCPI)
The merger route dodges India's anti-defection law
Opposition walked out of an all-party meet in protest over recognising them

depth.background: The Trinamool Congress (TMC), led by Mamata Banerjee, has run West Bengal for over a decade but suffered a heavy defeat in the state's 2026 Assembly elections. In India, lawmakers who switch parties normally lose their seats under the 'anti-defection law' (the Constitution's Tenth Schedule) — but there is a loophole: if two-thirds or more of a party's legislators merge as a bloc into another party, it counts as a legal merger, not defection.

stories.title: Madhya Pradesh clears a Uniform Civil Code draft that makes registering a live-in relationship compulsory — skip it, and you could face jail
stories.what_happened: On Sunday, July 19, the Madhya Pradesh cabinet approved a draft UCC bill. It criminalises triple talaq and 'nikah halala', prohibits polygamy, and makes couples register a live-in relationship within a month — with up to three months in jail or a ₹10,000 fine for failing to do so, and stiffer penalties for giving false information or ignoring a registrar's notice. It also gives children born from live-in relationships legal status and equal inheritance rights, lets an abandoned female partner claim maintenance, and exempts Scheduled Tribes. The bill will be introduced when the state assembly's session begins Monday.
key_points: MP cabinet approved the draft UCC bill on Sunday
Live-in of over a month without registering: up to 3 months' jail or ₹10,000 fine
Bans polygamy, triple talaq and 'nikah halala'; equal inheritance for all children
Scheduled Tribes are exempt; bill goes to the assembly Monday

depth.background: A Uniform Civil Code (UCC) is a single set of laws on marriage, divorce, inheritance and adoption that applies to everyone regardless of religion — replacing the separate 'personal laws' Hindus, Muslims, Christians and others currently follow. India's Constitution (Article 44) lists a UCC as a goal for the state to 'endeavour' towards, and the ruling BJP has long championed it; Uttarakhand became the first state to enact one. Madhya Pradesh, also BJP-run, is now moving to follow.

stories.title: After his Spa win, Antonelli's title lead balloons to 45 points — and Ferrari is fined for a pit-lane scare
stories.what_happened: With the Spa result confirmed, Antonelli extended his lead to 45 points over Hamilton, who reclaimed second place, while Russell dropped to 50 points behind after his DNF ('did not finish'). Separately, stewards fined Ferrari €30,000 (€10,000 suspended) for an 'unsafe release': during Hamilton's pit stop, a mechanic reaching in for a front-wing adjustment was struck by the car as it was waved off. The mechanic was unhurt, and because Hamilton stopped immediately and lost time, stewards issued a fine rather than a track penalty, so he keeps fourth.
key_points: Antonelli's championship lead over Hamilton grows to 45 points
Russell, out on lap one, is now 50 points adrift and fading
Ferrari fined €30,000 after Hamilton's car hit a mechanic in the pits
Stewards spared Hamilton a sporting penalty; he keeps 4th

depth.background: Kimi Antonelli, Mercedes' teenage star, has led the 2026 Formula 1 championship for much of the season, with team-mate George Russell his closest rival. At the Belgian Grand Prix at Spa-Francorchamps, Antonelli started on pole and won — his sixth victory of the year — while Russell retired on the opening lap after a collision with Ferrari's Lewis Hamilton. In F1, drivers score points by finishing position, and the season title goes to whoever accumulates the most across all races.

stories.title: India and the EU say their long-delayed free-trade deal is nearly done — aiming to sign by year-end and switch it on in early 2027
stories.what_happened: Commerce and Industry Minister Piyush Goyal concluded a July 13–18 visit to Spain, Belgium, Finland and Estonia, meeting EU leaders including European Council President Antonio Costa and Trade Commissioner Maros Sefcovic. Both sides reaffirmed their commitment to sign and implement the FTA early, and Goyal said the plan is to finalise the legal text before the end of 2026 and bring it into effect in the first quarter of 2027 (around February–March). Talks also covered an investment-protection agreement, market access for farm goods, Basmati rice, steel scrap, and the EU's carbon border tax.
key_points: Trade Minister Piyush Goyal wraps a five-day, four-nation Europe tour
Both sides reaffirm early signing and implementation of the FTA
Target: sign the legal text by end-2026, in force February–March 2027
Sticking points: farm goods, Basmati rice, steel scrap, EU's carbon border tax

depth.background: A free-trade agreement (FTA) is a deal to cut or scrap the taxes (tariffs) two sides charge on each other's goods, making trade cheaper and faster. India and the European Union — a 27-country bloc that is one of India's biggest trading partners — first started FTA talks back in 2007, then stalled for years over disagreements, and relaunched negotiations in 2022. After India recently sealed a trade pact with the UK, momentum has picked up.

stories.title: Bangladesh come from behind to clinch the T20 series in Zimbabwe — Tanzid Hasan's unbeaten 66 seals a nervy chase
stories.what_happened: Chasing Zimbabwe's 143 for 7, Bangladesh reached 144 for 6 with two balls to spare to win by four wickets and take the series 2-1 — their first T20I series win of 2026. Opener Tanzid Hasan held the innings together with an unbeaten 66 off 58 balls, his 13th T20I fifty. The finish was dramatic: an umpire's 'dead ball' call (ruling a delivery void because a fielder was moving illegally) denied Zimbabwe's Brad Evans a hat-trick, and Mahedi Hasan struck the winning boundary.
key_points: Bangladesh won the decider by 4 wickets to take the series 2-1
They fought back after losing the opening match
Tanzid Hasan anchored the chase with an unbeaten 66
A controversial 'dead ball' denied Zimbabwe's Evans a hat-trick

depth.background: Bangladesh and Zimbabwe were playing a three-match Twenty20 International (T20I) series in Bulawayo, Zimbabwe. T20 is cricket's shortest, fastest format — 20 overs a side — and a three-match series is decided by whoever wins two. Bangladesh had lost the first game, meaning the third and final match was a winner-takes-all decider.

stories.title: Antonelli wins the Belgian Grand Prix — holding off Leclerc as title rival Russell crashes out on lap one
stories.what_happened: Antonelli turned pole into victory, winning the 2026 Belgian Grand Prix for the first time — his sixth career win. He survived an opening-lap attack and an awkward Virtual Safety Car period, lost the lead briefly when Ferrari pitted Charles Leclerc at the right moment, then re-passed Leclerc on the Kemmel Straight on lap 34 to win. His championship rival and team-mate George Russell retired on the very first lap after contact with Lewis Hamilton, who was handed a 5-second penalty. Max Verstappen, the four-time champion, came home third.
key_points: Kimi Antonelli converts pole into his 6th career win at Spa
Passes Charles Leclerc on the Kemmel Straight on lap 34 to retake the lead
Title rival George Russell out on lap 1 after contact with Hamilton
Hamilton given a 5-second penalty; Verstappen finishes 3rd

depth.background: Formula 1 is the world's top motor-racing series: 20 drivers, 10 teams, points at every round, and a season-long fight for the drivers' championship. Kimi Antonelli, the 19-year-old Italian in a Mercedes, has emerged this year as the sport's breakout star and is locked in a title fight — including with his own Mercedes team-mate George Russell. Yesterday Antonelli took pole (the fastest qualifying lap, which earns the No. 1 starting spot) at Spa-Francorchamps, the long, fast, weather-prone circuit in the Belgian hills that drivers rate as the best on the calendar.

stories.title: Exam-leak anger reaches Parliament: opposition will demand Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan quit as the monsoon session opens tomorrow
stories.what_happened: Ahead of the monsoon session opening July 20, opposition parties signalled they will demand the resignation of Union Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan over the exam-leak scandal. Congress's B.K. Hariprasad, echoing Rahul Gandhi's 'Chhatron ki Goonj' campaign, alleged 152 paper leaks occurred between 2014 and 2026. Notably, Odisha's Naveen Patnaik (BJD) — often seen as non-aligned — urged the government to talk to protesting students, warning that 'a nation that compromises the integrity of its examination system compromises its future.' The government will lay out its legislative agenda at an all-party meeting. The session runs to August 13.
key_points: Opposition to target Education Minister over exam paper leaks
Congress cites '152 paper leaks' between 2014 and 2026
Even BJD's Naveen Patnaik urges talks with protesting students
Monsoon session runs July 20 to August 13

depth.background: For weeks, a youth-led movement has been building over repeated leaks of exam question papers — including for high-stakes tests like NEET, the national medical-entrance exam that decides who becomes a doctor. Leaks let some cheat while honest students lose seats, and the anger has spilled into street protests (the 'cockroach' march) and demands for accountability. That anger now moves from the streets into Parliament as the monsoon session begins.

stories.title: Iran hits Kuwait's water-and-power plants, forcing rationing — the Gulf war spreads to civilian lifelines
stories.what_happened: Early Saturday, July 18, a fire broke out at a second Kuwaiti power-generation and water-desalination plant after an Iranian strike — the second such facility hit within 24 hours. Kuwait's energy ministry took several generating units offline 'to preserve the safety of the facility and the stability of the grid' and urged residents to ration power at peak hours. Iran's Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) said it targeted US troop-deployment sites, launch pads and HIMARS rocket systems in Kuwait; Kuwait said the plants themselves were damaged. No casualties were reported, and by July 19 one plant was still burning.
key_points: Iran struck a second Kuwaiti power-and-desalination plant in 24 hours
Fire forced generating units offline; Kuwait told people to ration electricity
About 90% of Kuwait's drinking water comes from desalination
War with the US now hits Gulf civilians' basic services, not just bases

depth.background: The US and Iran have been in an escalating shooting war for over a week — the US bombing near Tehran, Iranian missiles killing US troops in Jordan. Kuwait is a small, oil-rich US ally on the Persian Gulf that hosts American forces, so Iran has been firing at US bases there and now at infrastructure. Crucially, Kuwait has almost no natural fresh water: it turns seawater into drinking water at big coastal desalination plants that also generate much of its electricity.

thread.previously: We reported the US had widened its air war — bombing near Tehran for the first time and disabling an Iranian tanker — as Iran fired back at US bases across the Gulf.
thread.changed: Iran's retaliation has now moved from US bases to civilian lifelines: it has hit two Kuwaiti water-desalination and power plants within 24 hours, forcing Kuwait to ration electricity.

prev_ref.title: US widens its war on Iran — bombing near Tehran for the first time and disabling a tanker, as Iran fires back at American bases in the Gulf

stories.title: Tate brothers arrested in Miami as UK seeks their extradition on 59 charges including rape and trafficking
stories.what_happened: US Marshals arrested the brothers on Saturday in Miami on a sealed warrant after British prosecutors announced a total of 59 charges and requested their extradition. Andrew Tate faces 42 charges including rape, human trafficking, indecent images of a child and assault; Tristan faces 17 including rape, sexual assault and trafficking. The UK's Crown Prosecution Service says the offences involve seven women in the East of England between 2010 and 2017. Andrew's lawyers say the claims are false and any activity was consensual.
key_points: US Marshals arrest Andrew and Tristan Tate in Miami on a sealed warrant
UK announces 59 charges tied to 7 alleged victims (2010–2017)
Andrew faces 42 charges, Tristan 17; both deny wrongdoing
Britain is now seeking their extradition from the US

depth.background: Andrew Tate is a former kickboxer turned online personality with a huge, mostly young-male following, known for 'hyper-masculine' content that critics call misogynistic. He and his brother Tristan have faced criminal investigations in several countries for years, including a separate case in Romania. This is the first time UK charges have led to their arrest on US soil.

stories.title: India's Gen Z 'cockroach' movement calls a march on Parliament — a youth revolt over exam leaks builds as the monsoon session opens
stories.what_happened: On Sunday, July 19, 2026, organisers confirmed a 'Chalo Sansad' ('Let's go to Parliament') march from Jantar Mantar to Parliament on Monday, July 20 — the first day of Parliament's monsoon session. The Cockroach Janta Party issued do's and don'ts (carry the national flag, no political-party flags, stay peaceful), and Wangchuk called it India's 'second freedom movement.' Their core demands: fix the exam system, act hard on paper leaks, and remove Union Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan. Delhi Police deployed extra forces and said an unauthorised march toward Parliament would not be allowed.
key_points: 'Chalo Sansad' (Let's go to Parliament) march set for July 20
Led by the youth 'Cockroach Janta Party' over exam-paper leaks
Demands: exam reform and the education minister's resignation
Follows activist Sonam Wangchuk's forced hospitalisation

depth.background: For years, India's students have been rocked by exam-paper 'leaks' — where the questions for high-stakes national tests get stolen and sold in advance, wrecking the results for millions who studied honestly. That anger fused with a broader Gen Z frustration into a loose youth movement that ironically calls itself the 'Cockroach Janta Party' — reclaiming an insult about ordinary people being crushed and ignored. Its most prominent backer, engineer-activist Sonam Wangchuk, has been on a hunger strike at Delhi's Jantar Mantar protest ground and was forcibly moved to a government hospital on Saturday.

thread.previously: Yesterday Delhi Police forcibly moved a fasting Sonam Wangchuk to a government hospital on day 21 of his hunger strike, and a court declined to order his release.
thread.changed: Today the youth movement behind him turned the anger into a mass 'Chalo Sansad' march on Parliament for July 20, with Wangchuk branding it a 'second freedom movement' and police vowing to block any unauthorised march.

prev_ref.title: Police forcibly move a fasting Sonam Wangchuk to hospital on day 21 of his Ladakh protest

stories.title: Hundreds of Israelis march on the Gaza border to demand Jewish resettlement — with coalition ministers backing them
stories.what_happened: Hundreds of activists marched from the town of Sderot toward the Gaza border on Sunday, July 19, demanding that Israel re-establish settlements inside Gaza. The military issued a 'closed military zone' order over parts of the western Negev and physically blocked marchers at roadblocks; organisers said a Likud minister joined the group as it pushed past an IDF checkpoint, and other senior coalition figures were expected to attend. The army and organisers negotiated so the crowd did not actually enter Gaza.
key_points: Marchers chanted 'Ours forever', demanding settlements be rebuilt in Gaza
The IDF declared the border a closed military zone and blocked them
Senior government ministers backed or joined the pro-settlement rally
It clashes with Israel's official stance that it won't resettle Gaza

depth.background: Israel withdrew all its settlers and soldiers from the Gaza Strip in 2005. A hard-right movement in Israel has long wanted to reverse that and rebuild Jewish settlements there — a goal that gained momentum during the long Gaza war. The march was organised by settler groups such as Nahala, and several ministers in Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's governing coalition openly support the idea.

stories.title: England pile up 387/3 in the Lord's ODI decider — India face a huge chase, and Rohit Sharma's future is on the line
stories.what_happened: England won the toss, chose to bat, and piled up a mammoth 387 for 3. Opener Ben Duckett smashed 141, and Joe Root became the first England batter to register six consecutive ODI half-centuries, a record run of form. India's bowlers had a chastening day, and India began their reply needing to chase down a target near 388 — one of the tougher chases in one-day cricket.
key_points: Series level 1-1; this third ODI at Lord's decides it
England batted first and posted a mammoth 387/3
Ben Duckett hit 141; Joe Root made a record 6th straight ODI fifty
India's chase — and captain Rohit Sharma's place — under the spotlight

depth.background: India and England are playing a three-match One-Day International (ODI) series — the 50-overs-a-side format. India won the first game, England the second, so the sides went to the final match at Lord's in London — cricket's most famous ground — tied 1-1, making it a winner-takes-all decider. Around it swirls a bigger question: the future of India's veteran captain Rohit Sharma, whose place in the one-day side has been debated as selectors look ahead.

thread.previously: England levelled the series 1-1 in Cardiff (guided by Joe Root's unbeaten 99), setting up a winner-takes-all third ODI at Lord's with Rohit Sharma's future in focus.
thread.changed: The decider is now underway: England batted first and posted a huge 387/3 (Duckett 141, Root a record 6th straight fifty), leaving India a daunting chase to win the series.

prev_ref.title: India vs England ODI series goes to a Lord's decider — with Rohit Sharma's future in the spotlight

stories.title: The talent-spotter who found Antonelli is leaving Mercedes for Red Bull
stories.what_happened: Multiple outlets report that Lagrue is set to leave Mercedes to take a similar talent-development role at Red Bull. It's an unusually visible transfer for a figure who normally works out of the spotlight, and it comes mid-season with Antonelli leading the championship for Mercedes.
key_points: Gwen Lagrue, Mercedes' junior-driver boss, set to join Red Bull
He ran Mercedes' young-driver programme since 2016
He is credited with discovering star rookie Kimi Antonelli
A rare high-profile move among F1's behind-the-scenes talent scouts

depth.background: In Formula 1, most attention goes to the drivers, but teams also compete fiercely for the people who find and develop future stars. Gwen Lagrue has led Mercedes' junior driver programme since 2016 and is widely credited with spotting Kimi Antonelli, the young Italian now fighting for the 2026 drivers' title.

stories.title: PV Sindhu wins the Japan Open — a first for any Indian, and her biggest title since 2019
stories.what_happened: On Sunday, July 19, 2026, Sindhu beat Japan's Akane Yamaguchi 21-17, 21-17 in the women's singles final in Tokyo to win the BWF Japan Open — becoming the first Indian to lift the title. The straight-games win over a home favourite ended a drought of more than two years and stands as her most significant title since the 2019 World Championships.
key_points: Beat home favourite Akane Yamaguchi 21-17, 21-17
First Indian ever to win the Japan Open singles title
Ends a title drought of more than two years
Her biggest crown since the 2019 World Championship

depth.background: PV Sindhu, 31, is India's most decorated badminton player — a two-time Olympic medallist and 2019 world champion — but she had gone through a long lean patch, without a top-tier tour title since the Syed Modi International in 2024. The Japan Open is a 'Super 750' event, one of the highest tiers on the world badminton tour (bigger prize money and ranking points mean the toughest fields), and no Indian had ever won its singles crown.

stories.title: A storm is spinning up in the Gulf of Mexico — forecasters give it an 80% chance of becoming a tropical cyclone within two days, aimed at Florida
stories.what_happened: On Sunday, July 19, the NHC said an area of low pressure over the Gulf near Florida has become better organised and now has an 80% chance of becoming a tropical cyclone within 48 hours. A tropical depression — the first, weakest stage of a named-storm system — is likely to form later Sunday or on Monday, and is expected to drift slowly north or northwest. Forecasters are warning of heavy rain and storm threats across Florida, Alabama, Mississippi and Louisiana.
key_points: US forecasters: 80% chance a cyclone forms within 48 hours
The low-pressure system sits in the Gulf near Florida, drifting north
Heavy rain and flood risk for Florida, Alabama, Mississippi, Louisiana
A tropical depression could form as early as Sunday or Monday

depth.background: The Atlantic hurricane season runs from June to November, and the warm, shallow Gulf of Mexico is one of the world's best incubators for tropical storms — bath-warm water is the fuel that lets a loose cluster of thunderstorms organise and spin. The US National Hurricane Center (NHC), a government forecasting office in Miami, tracks these systems and gives each a percentage chance of 'developing' into a named storm.

stories.title: Russia hits Kyiv with one of the biggest missile barrages of the war — 40+ missiles and 120+ drones
stories.what_happened: Overnight into Sunday, Russia fired more than 40 missiles of various types and about 120 attack drones, with most aimed at Kyiv, President Zelensky said. Strikes hit five districts of the capital, killing at least one person and wounding 16; across Ukraine at least 16 civilians were killed in 24 hours. Ukraine says it shot down 17 of the ballistic missiles and 108 drones.
key_points: Over 40 missiles and 120 drones, most aimed at Kyiv
At least 1 killed and 16 wounded in the capital
Includes 10 Zircon hypersonic missiles that are hard to shoot down
Ukraine says it downed 17 ballistic missiles, hinting at fresh Patriot stocks

depth.background: Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine has ground on for more than four years, and in recent weeks both sides have escalated long-range strikes on each other's cities and infrastructure. Just yesterday Ukraine launched its deadliest strike inside Russia in over two years, torching warehouses and an oil depot; this barrage is Russia's answer.

thread.previously: Yesterday we covered Ukraine's deadliest strike inside Russia in over two years, hitting a Wildberries logistics hub and an oil depot and killing at least 8.
thread.changed: Now Russia has retaliated with one of its biggest missile-and-drone barrages of the war against Kyiv, killing at least one and wounding 16 — the exchange is escalating, not cooling.

prev_ref.title: Ukraine lands its deadliest strike inside Russia in over two years: drones set Wildberries warehouses and a Moscow-region oil depot ablaze, killing at least 8

stories.title: A passenger ferry carrying 116 people capsizes off Guyana — a frantic overnight rescue pulls dozens from the water
stories.what_happened: Overnight into Sunday, July 19, 2026, the MV Barima — a ferry with 116 people aboard, travelling from the capital Georgetown toward Port Kaituma — capsized near Iron Punt off Guyana's coast. A distress call came in around 11:01pm local time, triggering a scramble of state and private boats. Prime Minister Mark Phillips said at least 53 people had been rescued; officials said the search for the rest was still going. The boat, built in 1939, was carrying 250 life jackets and several life rafts.
key_points: The MV Barima capsized on a Georgetown-to-Port Kaituma run
116 people aboard; more than 50 rescued so far
Distress call came around 11pm local time overnight
The vessel was built in 1939 — 87 years old

depth.background: Guyana is a small country on the northern coast of South America. Large parts of its interior have no roads, so government-run river-and-coastal ferries are a lifeline that ordinary people rely on to reach remote towns like Port Kaituma in the northwest. When one of those boats fails, there is often no quick backup, and help can be hours away by water.

stories.title: India's Parliament opens its monsoon session Monday — an all-party meeting today, and 7 contentious bills on the table
stories.what_happened: On Sunday, July 19, the government held the all-party meeting (around 11 a.m.) to outline its agenda. The session itself opens Monday, July 20, and runs to August 13 across 19 sittings. The government plans to push about seven bills — among them the Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Amendment Bill (which tightens the rules on foreign funding of NGOs and charities), a bill to raise the number of Supreme Court judges from 33 to 37 to clear a backlog of cases, and a Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSME) reform bill. The politically explosive 'delimitation' debate — over redrawing parliamentary seats — also looms over the session.
key_points: Monsoon session runs July 20 to Aug 13, with 19 sittings
Government holds the customary all-party meeting today
7 bills lined up, including FCRA and more Supreme Court judges
Expected to be stormy amid opposition splits and flashpoints

depth.background: India's Parliament meets in a few fixed sessions each year; the monsoon session is the mid-year one, running roughly July to August. By custom, just before it opens the government convenes an 'all-party meeting' — a sit-down with leaders of every party — to preview its legislative plan and take the temperature of the opposition.

stories.title: Lebanon's president heads to the White House with a plan to disarm Hezbollah — the first Lebanese leader to visit in nearly 20 years
stories.what_happened: Aoun is traveling to Washington for his first face-to-face meeting with US President Donald Trump, carrying a written proposal for how Hezbollah's large arsenal could be dismantled in stages, paired with a phased Israeli military withdrawal from two 'pilot zones' in the south. Ahead of the visit, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio publicly praised Aoun's 'courage' in trying to disarm Hezbollah and restore Lebanese sovereignty. Aoun's team says only Trump has the leverage to press Israel to pull back.
key_points: Aoun will hand Trump a written plan to decommission Hezbollah's weapons
In return he wants Israel to pull its troops out of south Lebanon
First Lebanese head of state at the White House in ~20 years
Hezbollah rejects any direct talks with Israel — the plan's biggest obstacle

depth.background: Hezbollah is an Iran-backed armed political movement in Lebanon that, for decades, kept a private army more powerful than the Lebanese state's own. A brutal 2024–2026 war with Israel badly degraded the group, and Israeli troops still occupy a strip of southern Lebanon. Joseph Aoun — a former commander of Lebanon's US-backed army — was elected president last year on a promise to reclaim the state's monopoly on force, meaning the government, not a militia, controls weapons.

stories.title: Government plans jail time for insulting 'Vande Mataram' — a new bill heads to Parliament as the monsoon session opens
stories.what_happened: Union Home Minister Amit Shah is set to introduce the Prevention of Insults to National Honour (Amendment) Bill, 2026 in the Rajya Sabha (Parliament's upper house) as the monsoon session opens (July 20 to August 13). If passed, intentionally insulting or obstructing the singing of Vande Mataram could bring up to three years in jail, a fine, or both — matching the protection given to the national anthem. It follows a July 9 Home Ministry directive that Vande Mataram be played before Jana Gana Mana at official government functions.
key_points: Amit Shah to introduce the bill in the Rajya Sabha as the monsoon session begins
Up to 3 years' jail, a fine, or both, for intentionally insulting or obstructing the national song
Would put Vande Mataram on par with the national anthem in law
Opposition (TMC, Congress) expected to resist it as coercive

depth.background: 'Vande Mataram' is India's national SONG — distinct from the national ANTHEM, 'Jana Gana Mana'. It has deep roots in the freedom movement but has long been politically contested, as some minority groups decline to sing it on religious grounds. India's existing law, the Prevention of Insults to National Honour Act, protects the flag, the constitution and the anthem — but not the national song.

stories.title: Drone strikes halt a major Black Sea oil pipeline's exports — a new front in the Russia-Ukraine war threatens oil supply
stories.what_happened: On Sunday, July 19, the CPC said oil loading at its Black Sea terminal was suspended after drone attacks on two tankers — the Asia and the Nissos Ios — while they were being loaded. The Asia caught fire, which was later controlled; the consortium reported no oil spill and no casualties, and shut moorings 1 and 3. CPC did not assign blame; a Chevron-linked tanker was among those hit.
key_points: Drones hit two tankers loading at the Caspian Pipeline's Black Sea terminal
CPC suspended loadings; it ships ~1.58 million barrels a day of mostly Kazakh crude
One tanker caught fire (later controlled); no spill or casualties reported
Attacks on Black Sea shipping have escalated sharply this past week

depth.background: The Caspian Pipeline Consortium (CPC) carries crude oil roughly 1,500 km from Kazakhstan's giant oilfields across southern Russia to an export terminal on Russia's Black Sea coast near Novorossiysk, where it's loaded onto tankers. It's the main export route for landlocked Kazakhstan and involves Western majors like Chevron and ExxonMobil. Russia and Ukraine have been trading escalating drone strikes, increasingly aimed at each other's ships and energy sites.

stories.title: Cuba frees dissident artist Luis Manuel Otero Alcántara after 5 years — on condition he leave the country, and he lands in Miami
stories.what_happened: Cuba released Otero Alcántara after his five-year sentence, but on the condition that he leave the country — a form of forced exile. The United States granted him parole (permission to enter), and he arrived in Miami, where a crowd cheered, sang and draped him in a Cuban flag bearing the words 'Patria y Vida'.
key_points: Cuban artist and 'Patria y Vida' co-writer released after a 5-year jail term
Freed on condition he go into exile; the US granted him parole
Greeted by a flag-waving crowd on arrival in Miami
Rights groups had long called him a political prisoner

depth.background: Luis Manuel Otero Alcántara, 38, is one of Cuba's best-known dissidents — a performance artist who co-founded the San Isidro Movement of Havana artists and writers, and who shared a Grammy for 'Patria y Vida' ('Homeland and Life'), a song that became an anthem for opponents of Cuba's one-party government. He was arrested during the mass anti-government protests of July 11, 2021, and in 2022 a court sentenced him to five years for 'public disorder', contempt and disrespecting national symbols. Amnesty International and others called him a political prisoner; Havana rejected the label.

stories.title: NEET score-mismatch row: NTA says some 'proof' sheets from aspirants are AI-generated fakes, warns of legal action
stories.what_happened: After the 2026 results, several aspirants alleged their declared scores were far lower than the answer sheets they had — one Pune student produced a downloaded OMR showing 707 marks versus a later one showing 217. The National Testing Agency (NTA), which conducts NEET, says that when it verified the complaints, many of the OMR sheets submitted as evidence appeared to be fabricated or created using AI. It has advised candidates and parents to submit only original OMR sheets and warned of legal action over fake documents.
key_points: Aspirants alleged big gaps between expected and declared NEET scores
One Pune student showed two sheets — 707 vs 217 marks
NTA says many OMR sheets submitted as proof are AI-generated fakes
NTA warns of legal action, tells candidates to submit only originals

depth.background: NEET-UG is India's single, high-stakes entrance exam for medical and dental colleges, taken by over 20 lakh (2 million) students a year, and answers are marked on OMR sheets read by machines. The exam has a troubled recent history of leak and integrity scandals, so any hint of score-tampering triggers intense anxiety among students and parents.

stories.title: India moves to add four more Supreme Court judges — a bill to fight the country's mountain of pending cases
stories.what_happened: As Parliament's monsoon session opens on July 20, the government plans to introduce the Supreme Court (Number of Judges) Amendment Bill, 2026, which would raise the sanctioned strength of Supreme Court judges from 33 to 37 (in addition to the Chief Justice of India), with the stated aim of reducing pendency. It is one of seven bills on the agenda, alongside an income-tax overhaul, an MSME (small-business) reform, and others — in a session expected to be stormy over unrelated flashpoints like exam leaks and party defections.
key_points: Bill would raise the Supreme Court's judge strength from 33 to 37
Goal: clear a huge backlog of pending cases faster
One of 7 bills the government will table in the monsoon session
Session opens July 20 amid heated opposition protests

depth.background: India's Supreme Court is the country's highest court, and the number of judges it can have is fixed by a law that Parliament can amend. For years the court has struggled with a swelling backlog — tens of thousands of cases wait years for a hearing — because there simply aren't enough judges to keep up with the flood of appeals in a country of 1.4 billion.

stories.title: A Kanpur engineering topper dies by suicide after nearly 50 failed tries at a government job — a note that reads 'Sorry, Papa'
stories.what_happened: Police in Kanpur, Uttar Pradesh, said Anand, a 23-year-old who had topped his B.Tech in Electrical Engineering (ranking third in the state and receiving a gold medal honoured by the Governor in 2024), was found hanging at his family home in the Gujaini area on Sunday. He left a note apologising to his father — 'Sorry, Papa' — and saying he had made nearly 50 attempts to secure a government job without success. His father said he had sat many recruitment exams and had been under severe stress for months.
key_points: Anand, 23, a B.Tech gold medallist, found dead at his Kanpur home
Suicide note said he made ~50 attempts to land a government job
Topped Electrical Engineering; ranked 3rd in UP; honoured by the Governor
A stark window into India's youth unemployment and exam pressure

depth.background: In much of India a stable government ('sarkari') job is prized above almost all else — for its security, pension and social status — so millions of graduates spend years cramming for fiercely competitive recruitment exams, where a handful of posts can draw lakhs of applicants. India's official unemployment is highest among the educated young, and repeated exam-paper leaks and delays (a grievance now fuelling the 'Cockroach' youth movement) have deepened the frustration.

stories.title: Delhi Police declare the 'Cockroach' party's July 20 march to Parliament illegal as a hospitalised Wangchuk refuses to break his fast
stories.what_happened: Delhi Police declared the CJP's planned July 20 march to Parliament illegal, saying no permission was sought or granted and warning that a crowd pushing toward the high-security Parliament zone would be stopped and could face detentions. The Delhi High Court declined a plea to discharge Wangchuk from the state-run hospital, and he is reported to be continuing his fast — refusing intravenous fluids, oral rehydration and medication. His wife, Gitanjali, has vowed to lead the march regardless.
key_points: Delhi Police call Monday's 'Sansad Chalo' march unauthorised, warn of detentions
Sonam Wangchuk, moved to hospital on day 21, keeps refusing fluids
His wife Gitanjali vows to lead the march anyway
Youth anger over exam leaks and jobs collides with the monsoon session opening

depth.background: Sonam Wangchuk is a Ladakhi education reformer and activist who has been on an indefinite hunger strike at Delhi's Jantar Mantar protest site, demanding accountability over exam irregularities (including in NEET, India's giant medical-entrance test) and constitutional safeguards for Ladakh. A youth movement styling itself the 'Cockroach Janata Party' (CJP) — a self-mocking name for a generation that says it survives everything — has rallied around him and called a 'Sansad Chalo' ('March to Parliament') for July 20, the day Parliament's monsoon session opens. On day 21 of his fast, police forcibly moved Wangchuk to Safdarjung Hospital.

thread.previously: In the early hours of Saturday, day 21 of his fast, Delhi Police moved Wangchuk from Jantar Mantar to Safdarjung Hospital, citing a court order.
thread.changed: The High Court has now declined to discharge him and he is refusing all fluids and medication, while police have branded the CJP's July 20 'Sansad Chalo' march illegal and his wife has vowed to lead it anyway.

prev_ref.title: Police forcibly move a fasting Sonam Wangchuk to hospital on day 21 of his Ladakh protest

stories.title: Monsoon fury batters India's north and northeast — a deadly cloudburst landslide in Nagaland, flash floods in Kashmir
stories.what_happened: On Sunday morning a cloudburst set off a massive landslide in Mon town in Nagaland, sweeping away around a dozen homes; at least eight people are feared dead and rescuers have recovered several bodies. Separately, flash floods in the Kashmir region killed at least 11, and rain-related incidents across north and east India pushed the toll higher. Multi-agency teams including the Army, Assam Rifles and the NDRF are searching in continuous rain.
key_points: Cloudburst triggers a landslide in Nagaland's Mon town, homes washed away
At least 8 feared dead in Mon; Kashmir flash floods kill 11
Rescue teams — Army, Assam Rifles, NDRF — dig through mud in the rain
IMD warns of more heavy rain across north and northeast India

depth.background: India's June-to-September monsoon brings the rain that farms depend on, but in the fragile, steep-sloped hills of the northeast and the Himalayas it also triggers cloudbursts, landslides and flash floods almost every year. This weekend a fresh burst of intense rain hit several states at once.

stories.title: A shallow earthquake in Peru's Andes kills at least five and flattens dozens of homes near Huancayo
stories.what_happened: On Saturday night, July 18 (early July 19 GMT), a quake struck the central Andes about 2 km from the city of Sicaya in Huancayo province, at a shallow depth of roughly 10 km. The US Geological Survey measured it at magnitude 5.5, while Peru's Geophysical Institute put it at 5.1. Authorities reported at least five people killed, more than 20 injured and around 300 left homeless, with about 48 houses destroyed and a local church and convent among the damaged buildings. The farming area of Chongo Bajo was among the hardest hit.
key_points: Quake struck Saturday night near Sicaya, in Huancayo province
USGS put the magnitude at 5.5; Peru's own agency measured 5.1
At least 5 dead, more than 20 injured, about 300 left homeless
A local church, a convent and roughly 48 houses were damaged or destroyed

depth.background: Peru sits on the Pacific 'Ring of Fire', where the Nazca tectonic plate under the ocean grinds beneath the South American plate — a slow-motion collision that builds up strain and releases it as frequent earthquakes. Many Andean towns are built from adobe (sun-dried mud brick), which is cheap but crumbles easily when the ground shakes.

stories.title: Europe's new digital border system is tripling airport queues — a summer-travel headache as fingerprints replace passport stamps
stories.what_happened: Airport bosses and airlines say the EES is sharply increasing wait times as staff enrol travellers' biometrics for the first time. At Rome's Fiumicino, average clearance for UK nationals has risen from about 7 minutes to 20, with occasional one-to-two-hour queues; Portugal's Faro and Spain's Barcelona have reported 30–60 minute waits. Ryanair has urged passengers to 'allow extra time' and blamed a 'failed EES rollout', while the European Commission says disruption at most airports is limited and it will keep supporting member states.
key_points: The EU's Entry/Exit System (EES) is slowing passport control for non-EU travellers
Border times up from ~7 to ~20 minutes at Rome; 1–2 hour queues reported
Non-EU visitors must now give fingerprints and a face scan on arrival
Ryanair tells passengers to allow extra time for the 'failed rollout'

depth.background: The Schengen Area is the group of 29 European countries that have abolished passport checks between one another, so a traveller crossing into any of them from outside is checked once at the external border. To tighten that border, the EU has been rolling out the Entry/Exit System (EES) since last October — a digital system that replaces the old ink passport stamp with a biometric record: non-EU arrivals register their fingerprints and a facial scan, which are then checked when they leave, to track who overstays.

stories.title: Britain's Josh Kerr breaks a 27-year-old mile world record — the first man ever under 3:43
stories.what_happened: On Saturday, July 18, 2026, at the Diamond League meet at London Stadium, British runner Josh Kerr ran 3:42.66 to break El Guerrouj's 27-year-old record by nearly half a second, becoming the first man ever to go under 3:43. Kerr had announced the attempt months earlier, dubbing it 'Project 222' (the number of seconds — 3:42 — he needed to beat). Two training partners set the pace, and he finished about three seconds clear of American Yared Nuguse.
key_points: Ran 3:42.66, first man to break the 3:43 barrier
Broke Hicham El Guerrouj's 1999 record by nearly half a second
Set at the Diamond League meet in London
Planned the record bid months ahead as 'Project 222'

depth.background: The mile is track's most storied distance — the event of Roger Bannister's first sub-4-minute run in 1954. Its world record had stood untouched since 1999, when Morocco's Hicham El Guerrouj ran 3 minutes 43.13 seconds, a mark so durable that many thought it might last a generation. The 'Diamond League' is the sport's top annual circuit of one-day meets, where the best runners chase fast times and prize money.

stories.title: India builds its own 'One Nation, One Time' network to stop relying on foreign GPS clocks
stories.what_happened: On Saturday, Union Minister Pralhad Joshi commissioned India's first high-precision network to distribute Indian Standard Time (IST) over fibre-optic/Ethernet lines, at the Regional Reference Standards Laboratory in Jakkur, Bengaluru. It uses 'White Rabbit' technology — a Precision Time Protocol method that syncs clocks to within billionths of a second — to send UTC(NPLI)-traceable IST securely to critical sectors. It was built by the Legal Metrology division with NPL, ISRO, state telecom BSNL and markets regulator SEBI, under a 'One Nation, One Time' vision.
key_points: New secure network distributes official Indian Standard Time over fibre
Cuts India's reliance on foreign GPS satellites for precise time
Aimed at banks, stock markets, telecom, power grids and defence
Uses 'White Rabbit' tech; commissioned in Jakkur, Bengaluru

depth.background: Modern systems don't just need the right date — they need the exact instant, synced across the country to fractions of a second. Stock trades, bank transfers, mobile networks, power grids and defence systems all depend on this. Today much of that timing comes from foreign satellite systems like the US GPS — which is convenient but a security risk: a foreign or hackable signal is a single point of failure. India has been pushing 'time sovereignty': controlling its own precise time using its national reference clock at the National Physical Laboratory (NPL).

stories.title: Iran's missiles kill 2 US troops in Jordan — the first American combat deaths of this war since spring
stories.what_happened: US Central Command (CENTCOM — the US military command that runs operations in the Middle East) said that as its forces defended against an Iranian ballistic-missile and drone attack, two US service members in Jordan were killed and one is missing. Four others were medically evacuated to hospitals in Jordan but have since been discharged. Jordan said it shot down 10 Iranian missiles fired into its airspace overnight into Saturday. The US is withholding the fallen troops' names until 24 hours after their families are told.
key_points: Iranian missiles and drones hit a US base in Jordan
2 US service members killed, 1 still missing
Jordan says it intercepted 10 Iranian missiles overnight
Comes on the 7th straight night of US strikes on Iran

depth.background: For more than a week the United States and Iran have been in open military conflict. The US has been bombing Iranian military sites nightly — including near the capital, Tehran — and Iran has been firing back at American forces and US allies across the Gulf. Jordan is a close US partner in the region and hosts American troops on its soil, which put it in the line of fire.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported the US had widened its air war — striking near Tehran for the first time and disabling an Iranian tanker — while Iran fired back at American bases across the Gulf.
thread.changed: Now Iran's strikes have killed US personnel for the first time in this war since spring: two service members died and one is missing after missiles and drones hit a base in Jordan, and Jordan says it shot down 10 incoming missiles.

prev_ref.title: US widens its war on Iran — bombing near Tehran for the first time and disabling a tanker, as Iran fires back at American bases in the Gulf

stories.title: Chennamma, wife of former PM H.D. Deve Gowda and matriarch of a political dynasty, dies at 85 in Bengaluru
stories.what_happened: Chennamma Deve Gowda, 85, died on the evening of Saturday, July 18, 2026, at Manipal Hospital in Bengaluru. She had been admitted about four days earlier with breathing difficulties and, doctors said, died of a severe heart attack. She is survived by her husband, four sons and two daughters. Condolences poured in from across the political spectrum, including from Karnataka CM Siddaramaiah and Congress leader Rahul Gandhi.
key_points: Chennamma Deve Gowda died of a heart attack at 85
Wife of former PM and JD(S) patriarch H.D. Deve Gowda
Mother of six, including Union minister H.D. Kumaraswamy
Leaders across parties, including Rahul Gandhi, condoled

depth.background: H.D. Deve Gowda served as India's Prime Minister in 1996–97 and is the founder-patriarch of the Janata Dal (Secular), or JD(S), a Karnataka-based party that has repeatedly held the balance of power in the state. Chennamma, whom he married in 1954, was the quiet anchor of a large political family — their son H.D. Kumaraswamy is a former Karnataka Chief Minister and current Union minister, and other relatives hold elected office across the state.

stories.title: Ukraine's leadership is in turmoil mid-war: Zelensky sacked his star defence minister, crowds are protesting for a third day, and his top general may be next
stories.what_happened: On July 15 Zelensky removed Fedorov, the popular defence minister, as part of a wider government reshuffle that also replaced the prime minister. That triggered protests: crowds gathered in Kyiv and other cities for a third consecutive day on July 18, with two demands — reinstate Fedorov and sack Syrskyi. Reporting (FT, Bloomberg) says Zelensky is now considering replacing Syrskyi too, with interviews for candidates expected over the July 18–19 weekend. Ukraine is currently fighting under an interim defence chief.
key_points: Zelensky fired 35-year-old defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov
Thousands protested in Kyiv for a third straight day
Protesters want Fedorov back — and army chief Syrskyi gone
A rare public rift over how to fight the war

depth.background: Ukraine has been fighting Russia's full-scale invasion for nearly four and a half years. For most of that time the country projected unity, but a quiet fight has been building at the top over HOW to fight: Fedorov, a young tech-first minister, is credited with the cheap drones and unmanned systems that have let Ukraine punch above its weight, while 60-year-old Commander-in-Chief Oleksandr Syrskyi — a general trained in the Soviet era — favours classic, centralised command: more infantry, hold ground. The two clashed, and Zelensky sided with his general.

stories.title: The World Cup final is here: Spain vs Argentina, Messi vs Yamal — the planet's biggest match, tonight in New Jersey
stories.what_happened: Spain beat France 2-0 and Argentina came from behind to beat England 2-1 to set up the final at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey, on Sunday, July 19, kicking off around 3pm US Eastern time (past midnight in India) before a crowd of 80,000-plus. It pits the tournament's most prolific attack (Argentina, 19 goals) against its stingiest defence (Spain, one goal conceded). Messi leads the scoring charts with eight goals and is favourite for the Golden Boot (top-scorer award). A widely-cited statistical model, Opta's, gives Spain a roughly 59% chance to win.
key_points: Final tonight at MetLife Stadium, New York/New Jersey, ~3pm US ET
Messi (39), defending champion, meets Spain's teen star Yamal (19)
Argentina's top attack (19 goals) vs Spain's best defence (1 conceded)
Opta model favours Spain ~59%; Argentina chase back-to-back titles

depth.background: The FIFA World Cup is football's biggest tournament, held every four years; the 2026 edition is the first hosted jointly by the US, Canada and Mexico and the first with 48 teams. Argentina are the defending champions (they won in 2022) and hold the Copa America, South America's title; Spain are the reigning European champions. Lionel Messi, 39, is one of the greatest players ever and is playing what is almost certainly his last World Cup; Lamine Yamal, 19, is Spain's breakout superstar — and a viral 2007 photo shows a young Messi helping bathe Yamal as a baby, making tonight a literal changing-of-the-guard.

stories.title: Hungary removes its president — Orban loyalist Sulyok agrees to go, defusing a constitutional standoff
stories.what_happened: On July 13 Hungary's parliament passed a constitutional amendment — by 139 votes to 6 — to end Sulyok's term immediately. For a president's removal to take effect, that law must be signed, and Sulyok at first refused, calling the whole thing unconstitutional and raising the prospect of impeachment and a constitutional crisis. On Saturday, July 18, under a signing deadline, he relented and agreed to stand down, ending the standoff.
key_points: Parliament voted to remove President Tamas Sulyok, an Orban ally
He resisted for days, calling the move unconstitutional
On July 18 he agreed to stand down, averting a crisis
Follows Viktor Orban's landslide election loss in April

depth.background: Viktor Orban ran Hungary for 16 years until he lost power in April 2026 to Peter Magyar's Tisza Party in a landslide. Tamas Sulyok, the country's president — a largely ceremonial head-of-state role — was widely seen as an Orban loyalist left over from the old order. The new government moved to remove him and complete the changing of the guard.

stories.title: Canada's record wildfires push New York and Washington to 'most polluted' — as air turns hazardous across the US Northeast and Midwest
stories.what_happened: Thick haze from more than 800 active Canadian wildfires — including a dozen recent flare-ups in Ontario — pushed New York City and Washington, DC into the ranks of the world's most polluted major cities on Saturday. Washington was placed under a 'Code Purple' alert, meaning the air was 'very unhealthy' for everyone, and parts of northeast Minnesota reached 'hazardous' levels. Air-quality alerts stretched from Minnesota to the East Coast. Canadian officials said fire activity in Ontario is beginning to ease and expect air to improve.
key_points: 800+ wildfires burning across Canada this summer
Smoke pushed NYC and Washington DC into world's most-polluted cities
Washington hit 'Code Purple' — very unhealthy for everyone
Officials say Ontario fires are easing; air should improve

depth.background: Canada is having another catastrophic fire season — about 3,500 fires have burned more than 6 million acres this summer, driven by hot, dry conditions that scientists link to a warming climate. Smoke from these fires doesn't stay put: high-altitude winds carry it hundreds of miles south, blanketing US cities. This has become a near-yearly pattern, and it flared into a political row a day earlier when President Trump threatened new tariffs on Canada over the 'filthy' air.

thread.previously: Yesterday we covered the political angle: Trump calling Canada's drifting wildfire smoke 'Willful Negligence' and threatening tariffs, with Canada's PM Carney saying both nations share responsibility for the climate change driving the fires.
thread.changed: Today the story is the health emergency itself — smoke pushed New York and Washington into the world's most-polluted cities, Washington hit 'Code Purple,' and parts of Minnesota reached hazardous air, though officials now say Ontario's fires are easing.

prev_ref.title: Trump threatens to make Canada pay for its wildfire smoke — through tariffs — as 'filthy' haze from record fires chokes US cities

stories.title: Venezuela's earthquake toll tops 5,000 as the IMF rushes in emergency aid — and reports reveal a botched military rescue
stories.what_happened: On Saturday, July 18, 2026, Venezuelan authorities said the number of confirmed dead from the June quakes had risen above 5,000, with tens of thousands injured and roughly 17,000-plus people left homeless. The International Monetary Fund (IMF) — the global lender that governments turn to in a financial emergency — released about £346 million (roughly $460 million) in emergency funds to help pay for relief and rebuilding. At the same time, a Reuters investigation reported that Venezuela's military response was hobbled in the crucial first hours: delayed orders from senior commanders, a lack of basic equipment, and general confusion slowed the deployment of troops when speed mattered most. Officials estimate around 590,000 buildings were destroyed, and a UN agency has put the total damage near $37 billion.
key_points: Death toll from June 24's twin quakes now passes 5,000, still climbing
IMF releases ~£346m (about $460m) in emergency funds to Venezuela
Reuters: delayed orders and missing gear crippled the army's first-hours response
~590,000 buildings destroyed; UN pegs the damage at roughly $37 billion

depth.background: On June 24, 2026, two huge earthquakes — magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 — struck western Venezuela within seconds of each other, one of the deadliest natural disasters in the country's modern history. Venezuela was already a broken economy before the ground shook: years of mismanagement, hyperinflation and US sanctions had hollowed out the state, driven millions to emigrate, and left public services and building standards badly weakened. That matters because how deadly a quake becomes depends less on its raw power than on the quality of the buildings it hits and the government's ability to dig people out fast — and Venezuela was short on both. In the weeks since, the confirmed death toll has climbed relentlessly as rescuers reach flattened towns: from about 3,535 on July 7 to 4,333, and now past 5,000.

stories.title: Antonelli beats Verstappen to pole at the Belgian GP as Mercedes keeps dominating qualifying
stories.what_happened: At the Belgian Grand Prix at Spa-Francorchamps, Antonelli clinched his sixth pole position of the season, edging four-time champion Max Verstappen by 0.317 seconds. Verstappen admitted he only reached the front row thanks to a 'tow' — a slipstream — from Red Bull team-mate Isack Hadjar, who runs at the back after engine-change penalties. Reigning champion Lando Norris will drop 10 places on the grid for using too many engine parts, and Lewis Hamilton crashed his Ferrari in final practice earlier in the day.
key_points: Kimi Antonelli takes his 6th pole of 2026
Beat Max Verstappen by 0.317s at Spa
Verstappen aided by a team-mate's slipstream
Champion Lando Norris hit with a 10-place grid penalty

depth.background: Formula 1's qualifying decides the starting order for Sunday's race, and starting up front is a big advantage on tracks where overtaking is hard. Italian teenager Kimi Antonelli, in only his second F1 season with Mercedes, leads the 2026 drivers' championship, and Mercedes cars have been fastest over one lap all year.

stories.title: A top German conservative quits over a surrogacy row — Jens Spahn steps down after backing a ban he then used abroad
stories.what_happened: News broke on Thursday that Spahn and his husband had become parents through a surrogate in the United States. That triggered accusations of hypocrisy — because his party opposes legalising surrogacy at home — and on Saturday, July 18, Spahn announced he was stepping down as the CDU/CSU's parliamentary leader. In his resignation letter he wrote that his 'personal happiness — starting a family with my husband and becoming a father — is incompatible with my political office.'
key_points: Spahn resigns as CDU/CSU parliamentary floor leader — a very senior post
He and his husband became parents via a surrogate in the US
Surrogacy is banned in Germany; his party reaffirmed the ban in February
Critics called it hypocrisy; he cited family life as incompatible with the job

depth.background: Jens Spahn, 46, is a leading figure in Germany's Christian Democratic Union (CDU) and its Bavarian sister party the CSU — the conservative bloc that governs under Chancellor Friedrich Merz. Spahn, a former health minister, held one of the country's most powerful parliamentary jobs: leader of the CDU/CSU group in the Bundestag (Germany's parliament), which effectively runs the governing bloc's business on the floor. Surrogacy — where a woman carries a baby for someone else — is prohibited in Germany, though it is legal to raise a child born via surrogacy abroad, and the CDU voted to uphold that domestic ban at its party conference in February 2026.

stories.title: The backlash against AI's power-hungry data centres goes national — protests planned in 125+ US towns
stories.what_happened: A grassroots group called HumansFirst — co-founded by a former Tea Party organiser — coordinated protests in at least 125 locations across the US on Saturday, the first nationwide effort to channel local anger at the data-centre buildout. Organisers rail against what they call an 'unaccountable' expansion and an 'infringement on liberty.' Water is a flashpoint: one proposed project in California's Imperial County could draw about 260 million gallons a year from the Colorado River. A June Reuters/Ipsos poll found only about a third of Americans approve of the pace of construction, and just 14% would welcome one in their own community.
key_points: First coordinated national protest against the AI data-centre boom
Rallies planned in at least 125 US locations on Saturday
Anger centres on water and electricity use, and lack of local say
Only ~14% of Americans want a data centre in their own community

depth.background: The AI boom runs on 'data centres' — giant warehouses packed with computer servers that train and run models like ChatGPT and Claude. Over the past year, tech giants (Meta, Alphabet/Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Elon Musk's xAI) have raced to build them across the US. They guzzle electricity and, often, huge volumes of water for cooling, and they've been sprouting up faster than many towns can weigh in — turning a national tech story into thousands of local fights.

stories.title: A stomach-bug outbreak pulls Taylor Farms lettuce off US shelves — and Taco Bell's menu
stories.what_happened: US health agencies (the CDC and FDA) linked shredded iceberg lettuce served at Taco Bell to a Cyclospora outbreak first confirmed in five states — Indiana, Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio and West Virginia — and traced it to a single grower in central Mexico. On July 17, supplier Taylor Farms de México said it was voluntarily removing all iceberg lettuce sourced from central Mexico from the US market, and Taco Bell said it had pulled the lettuce from restaurants and its nationwide supply chain. Reporting suggests the wider outbreak may have sickened thousands, with cases being investigated across dozens of states.
key_points: Shredded iceberg lettuce tied to a Cyclospora outbreak
Taylor Farms recalls all central-Mexico iceberg lettuce
Taco Bell removes the lettuce nationwide
Thousands possibly sickened across many states

depth.background: Cyclospora is a tiny parasite that contaminates fresh produce, usually via water tainted with human waste, and causes cyclosporiasis — days or weeks of watery diarrhoea, cramps and fatigue. It can't be washed off reliably and isn't killed by refrigeration, so the main fix once produce is contaminated is to pull it from the food chain. The US sees these outbreaks most summers, often traced to imported salad greens.

stories.title: Iraq signs big US energy deals to reroute its oil around the Strait of Hormuz
stories.what_happened: At a US-Iraq business summit at the US Chamber of Commerce in Washington on Friday, the two sides signed preliminary agreements worth more than $60bn spanning energy, healthcare and technology. The headline deal is a plan for US giant Chevron to help rebuild the long-defunct pipeline from Iraq's oil-rich Kirkuk region to Syria's Mediterranean port of Baniyas — letting Iraqi crude reach world markets without going near Hormuz. Chevron signed two further oil-production deals, and Iraq also formally signed up Elon Musk's Starlink satellite internet.
key_points: US-Iraq summit yields $60bn+ in initial deals
Centrepiece: revive the old Iraq-to-Syria oil pipeline
Goal: stop depending on the war-hit Strait of Hormuz
Chevron to lead; Iraq also signs up Starlink

depth.background: Roughly a fifth of the world's oil is shipped through the Strait of Hormuz, a narrow sea passage between Iran and Oman — a classic 'chokepoint' where a single conflict can throttle global supply. With the US now fighting a war against Iran, tankers passing Hormuz face growing risk, and oil-exporting neighbours like Iraq are scrambling for safer routes to sell their crude.

stories.title: The US fills all 85,000 H-1B work visas — and for the first time picks winners by salary, not luck, hitting India's junior tech hopefuls hardest
stories.what_happened: On July 17, 2026, US Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) announced it had received enough petitions to fill the entire 85,000 H-1B cap for fiscal year 2027 and would not hold a second lottery — closing the door for anyone not already selected. Crucially, FY2027 is the first cycle run under the new wage-based selection: instead of everyone getting one equal ticket, applicants are entered according to the US Labor Department's four prevailing-wage tiers — a Level 4 (highest-paid) job gets four entries, Level 3 gets three, Level 2 gets two, and Level 1 (entry-level) gets just one. The effect was dramatic: total registrations fell 38.5%, from about 343,981 for FY2026 to roughly 211,600 for FY2027, as low-wage and speculative filings dried up. Among those selected, 71.5% now hold a US advanced degree (up from 57%), and only 17.7% fell in the lowest wage band.
key_points: USCIS says the FY2027 H-1B cap of 85,000 is full — no second lottery this year
First year selection is 'wage-weighted': higher-paid roles get more lottery entries
Registrations crashed 38.5% — from ~343,981 to ~211,600 — as the odds shifted
Indians win ~72% of H-1Bs, so the biggest single user of the visa is most exposed

depth.background: The H-1B is America's main work visa for skilled foreign professionals — the route most Indian software engineers, data scientists and consultants use to work in the US. Demand far outstrips supply: Washington caps new H-1Bs at 85,000 a year (65,000 general plus 20,000 for holders of US master's degrees or higher), and for years the winners were chosen by a random lottery among registrations. Indians are by far the biggest users — roughly 72% of H-1B holders in 2023–24 — and Indian IT giants like TCS, Infosys, Wipro and HCL long built their US business on it (though they have already nearly halved their H-1B workforce since 2017). This year the rules changed: the US Department of Homeland Security replaced the pure coin-flip with a 'wage-weighted' system.

stories.title: Speaker lets 6 Uddhav Thackeray MPs defect to Shinde's Shiv Sena — a fresh blow to the UBT camp
stories.what_happened: Lok Sabha Speaker Om Birla on Saturday formally recognised the merger of six Shiv Sena (UBT) MPs into the Shinde-led Shiv Sena. Because six of UBT's nine MPs crossed over — two-thirds — the move qualifies under the merger exception. That leaves Uddhav Thackeray's faction with just three MPs in the lower house of Parliament, while Shinde's group rises to 13. The decision, taken two days before Parliament's Monsoon Session, also lifts the BJP-led National Democratic Alliance's Lok Sabha strength to 298.
key_points: Lok Sabha Speaker recognises the 6 MPs' switch
Shinde's Sena now has 13 MPs; Uddhav left with 3
Cleared under the anti-defection law's 'merger' rule
Lifts the ruling NDA's Lok Sabha tally to 298

depth.background: The Shiv Sena, a big Maharashtra party, split in 2022 when Eknath Shinde broke away with most lawmakers and, backed by the BJP, took over the government — leaving founder Bal Thackeray's son Uddhav with a smaller faction, officially called Shiv Sena (UBT). India's anti-defection law normally bars lawmakers from switching parties to stop horse-trading, but it makes an exception: if two-thirds of a party's legislators agree to 'merge' with another party, the switch is legal and they keep their seats.

stories.title: A fire tears through a Norwegian town and becomes the country's largest in modern times — 100+ homes gone, hundreds evacuated
stories.what_happened: Police say a fire broke out in a townhouse in Drammen at about 3:30 p.m. on Friday. It spread rapidly through the densely built neighbourhood and into adjacent forest, destroying more than 100 homes — which officials call the largest fire of its kind in Norway's modern history. Hundreds of residents were evacuated to a shelter, and NRK, Norway's public broadcaster, reported no one missing. Firefighters were still fighting to contain it on Saturday, and the cause was not yet known.
key_points: Fire started in a townhouse in Drammen, southern Norway, on Friday afternoon
It jumped house-to-house and into nearby forests — 100+ homes destroyed
Hundreds fled to an evacuation centre; no one reported missing
Cause still unknown; crews battled the blaze into Saturday

depth.background: Norway is a cool, wet Nordic country not known for the kind of mass wildfires seen in California or Australia — big residential fires there are rare, which is exactly why this one is historic. Drammen is a city of about 100,000 people roughly 40 km southwest of the capital, Oslo. A dry, warm spell across parts of Northern Europe this summer left vegetation and timber homes unusually flammable.

stories.title: India vs England ODI series goes to a Lord's decider — with Rohit Sharma's future in the spotlight
stories.what_happened: England levelled the series 1-1 by winning the second ODI in Cardiff, guided by Joe Root's unbeaten 99. That sets up a winner-takes-all third ODI at Lord's, cricket's most famous ground. Amid swirling retirement rumours, Rohit turned up for India's optional practice and looked in good touch, while India named all-rounder Harsh Dubey to replace the injured Washington Sundar, opening the door for spinner Kuldeep Yadav's possible return. India have not won a bilateral ODI series in England since 2018.
key_points: Series level 1-1 after England win in Cardiff
Decider at Lord's; India seek first series win in England since 2018
Rohit Sharma under retirement speculation, joins optional practice
Harsh Dubey replaces injured Washington Sundar

depth.background: India and England are playing a three-match one-day international (ODI) series — the 50-overs-a-side format. Veteran opener Rohit Sharma, 39, was recently removed as India's ODI captain and has struggled for runs, fuelling talk that this could be one of his last outings and that younger opener Yashasvi Jaiswal is ready to replace him.

stories.title: India's National Film Awards for 2024 announced — Kartik Aaryan and Mammootty share Best Actor, Yami Gautam Best Actress
stories.what_happened: On July 18 the ministry announced winners across 43 feature and non-feature categories. The Best Actor award was shared by Kartik Aaryan (for the sports biopic Chandu Champion) and Mammootty (for the Malayalam horror film Bramayugam). Yami Gautam won Best Actress for Article 370, which was also named Best Film. Nag Ashwin's big-budget science-fiction epic Kalki 2898 AD won Best Popular Film Providing Wholesome Entertainment, and Randeep Hooda won Best Debut Director for Swatantrya Veer Savarkar. An 11-member jury headed by filmmaker Jayaraj made the picks.
key_points: 72nd National Film Awards announced for films of 2024
Kartik Aaryan (Chandu Champion) & Mammootty (Bramayugam) share Best Actor
Yami Gautam wins Best Actress for Article 370
Article 370 named Best Film; Kalki 2898 AD wins Best Popular Film

depth.background: The National Film Awards are India's highest government honours for cinema, handed out by the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting and decided by an appointed jury. The 72nd edition covers films released during 2024. Winning one carries prestige, career weight, and — because a government body picks them — plenty of scrutiny over what gets celebrated.

stories.title: Kotak Mahindra Bank's profit jumps about 26% — another solid quarter for India's private banks
stories.what_happened: On July 18, Kotak reported standalone net profit of about ₹4,123 crore for the June quarter, up roughly 26% from a year earlier and ahead of forecasts. On a consolidated basis (adding its insurance, broking and other arms) profit was about ₹5,480 crore. Total income rose ~12.6% to about ₹30,069 crore, net advances (loans) grew ~15%, and the gross NPA (bad-loan) ratio held steady at 1.18%.
key_points: Standalone net profit ~₹4,123 crore for the April–June quarter, up ~26%
Consolidated profit ~₹5,480 crore; total income up ~12.6%
Loans (net advances) grew ~15%; bad-loan ratio steady at 1.18%
Results beat analysts' estimates

depth.background: Kotak Mahindra Bank is one of India's largest private-sector banks. These are results for the April–June quarter — the first quarter of financial year 2026-27 (FY27) — the same reporting season in which HDFC, ICICI and Axis just posted numbers, so together they're a key read on how India's lenders and borrowers are doing.

stories.title: Under fire over its voter-list clean-up, India's Election Commission goes on the offensive to defend its credibility
stories.what_happened: Facing a growing row, the ECI has launched what commentators are calling a 'credibility offensive' to defend the process. Chief Election Commissioner Gyanesh Kumar has argued that the SIR has made voter lists more accurate and reliable and that India's electoral system is among the most transparent and credible in the world. Critics disagree sharply: civil-society coalitions claim that across the states and union territories where SIR has run, several crore voters risk being wrongly left off the rolls. The house-to-house verification phase is scheduled to run from June 30 to July 29, 2026.
key_points: EC pushes back as the SIR voter-roll revision draws heavy criticism
CEC says SIR made rolls more accurate and India's polls more credible
Critics say the drive risks wrongly deleting eligible voters
House-to-house verification is running June 30–July 29

depth.background: The Election Commission of India (ECI) is the independent body that runs the country's elections and maintains the electoral rolls — the official lists of who is allowed to vote. It has been carrying out a 'Special Intensive Revision' (SIR): a door-to-door exercise to update those lists by removing people who have died, moved away, are duplicates or are not citizens, while trying not to drop genuine voters. The drive has become one of India's most contested political issues, because opposition parties and civil-society groups fear that aggressive deletions could strip real voters — often the poor or migrants — off the rolls before elections.

stories.title: Germany raises its national terror-threat level to 'high' — and moves to hand its spy agencies more power
stories.what_happened: On Saturday, July 18, 2026, German Interior Minister Alexander Dobrindt told a newspaper that he was upgrading the country's security posture from the long-standing 'abstract' threat level to a 'high threat level'. He said 'the increased volume of reporting and intelligence' had driven the decision, and that Germans must now 'reckon with the risk of attacks at all times'. Officials indicated the danger is not vague: attack plans are 'clearly identifiable' and could be aimed at critical infrastructure (power, water, transport), specific institutions, or named individuals — with one plot reportedly involving 'disposable actors' using explosives that law enforcement disrupted. The announcement comes as Merz's coalition prepares to expand the operational powers of Germany's intelligence services so they can act faster and cooperate more closely with foreign partners.
key_points: Interior minister lifts the alert from 'abstract' to a 'high threat level'
Says intelligence points to identifiable, credible attack plans right now
Warns targets could include infrastructure, institutions and named individuals
Merz government prepares to expand the intelligence services' powers

depth.background: A country's 'threat level' is an official gauge of how likely a terrorist attack is judged to be, based on what its security agencies are picking up — intercepted chatter, tip-offs, foiled plots and intelligence shared by allies. Germany, Europe's largest economy and most populous country, has lived with a persistent 'abstract' risk for years — meaning attacks were considered possible but no specific, imminent plot stood out. In recent years it has suffered several deadly incidents, including car-ramming and knife attacks, and its agencies have repeatedly warned that Islamist militants, lone actors radicalised online, and sabotage crews linked to foreign states are all active threats. Chancellor Friedrich Merz's government has been pushing to modernise and strengthen the country's domestic intelligence apparatus in response.

stories.title: PNB's profit 'triples' to ₹5,253 crore — but the eye-popping jump is mostly a tax quirk, not a business boom
stories.what_happened: PNB's net profit jumped about 214% from a year earlier to ₹5,253 crore (roughly $630 million), up from ₹1,675 crore. The main reason was a far lower income-tax bill: PNB set aside ₹1,725 crore for tax this quarter versus ₹5,083 crore a year ago, when it had booked a large one-time tax charge after switching to a new tax regime. Its core lending income — net interest income, the difference between interest earned on loans and paid on deposits — rose only about 3%. Asset quality improved, with gross bad loans (NPAs) falling to 2.78% of loans from 3.78% a year earlier.
key_points: Q1 net profit up ~214% year-on-year to ₹5,253 crore
Real driver: a much smaller income-tax charge than last year
Core income (NII) rose only about 3% — the modest bit
Bad loans improved: gross NPAs fell to 2.78% from 3.78%

depth.background: Punjab National Bank (PNB) is one of India's largest public-sector (government-owned) banks. On Saturday, July 18, it reported results for the April-June quarter (the first quarter of financial year 2026-27). Company profits can swing sharply for reasons that have little to do with day-to-day business — especially taxes and one-off charges — so a huge headline percentage always needs a second look.

stories.title: At least 8 killed as an illegal firecracker factory explodes and burns in Ahmedabad — the latest deadly blast in India's loosely policed fireworks trade
stories.what_happened: On Saturday, July 18, 2026, a fire broke out at the Talent Fireworks factory on Gatrad Road in the Mahmudpura area near Ahmedabad, Gujarat. A series of powerful explosions ripped through the unit and shook the surrounding neighbourhood, throwing up a huge blaze; at least eight workers were killed and more than 15 injured, several critically. Police said the factory was operating illegally, without the mandatory licences and safety clearances. Gujarat Chief Minister Bhupendra Patel expressed grief and announced compensation of ₹4 lakh (about ₹400,000) to the family of each person killed and ₹50,000 to each of the injured, with the state arranging medical treatment for the survivors.
key_points: Blasts then a blaze tore through the Talent Fireworks unit on Gatrad Road
At least 8 dead, 15+ injured; the factory was operating illegally
Explosions rocked the Mahmudpura area near Ahmedabad, sparking panic
Gujarat CM announces ₹4 lakh for each victim's family, ₹50,000 for injured

depth.background: India is one of the world's biggest makers and users of firecrackers, and much of that production happens in small, cramped workshops — many of them unlicensed — where workers handle highly explosive chemicals by hand with little safety gear. Because these units store large amounts of gunpowder-like material in tight spaces, a single spark can set off a chain of explosions, so fatal fires at such factories are tragically common. Gatrad, on the industrial outskirts of Ahmedabad in Gujarat, is one such pocket of these small units.

stories.title: India's biggest bank grows the slowest: HDFC Bank's profit crawls up just 5% to ₹19,060 crore as its lending margin hits a record low
